summary: Uncle Bob recounts a frustrating restaurant experience where a POS terminal wouldn't allow a waiter to order two of the same sausage type, illustrating how software often constrains rather than enables users. He argues that while early software's inflexibility was forgivable given the novelty of automation, modern users rightfully expect software to get out of their way. The post challenges programmers to take personal responsibility for usability, not hiding behind UX teams or specifications. Developers should actively play with their software, invent use cases, and find constraints before users do.
key_points: Software should be more flexible than the manual processes it replaces, not more restrictive
The era of forgiving inflexible software is over—users now expect technology to adapt to them
Programmers are stakeholders whose reputation rides on the product's success
Developers should actively test their software as users would, inventing edge cases rather than blindly implementing specs
UX is everyone's responsibility, regardless of job title
money_quotes: Software is supposed to make life easier. Software is supposed to ease and enable the job of users.
All too often, however, software is constraining and restrictive. If you don't do what the software expects, the software fights you and stops you.
Nowadays we expect software to get out of our way.
That POS terminal should not get in the way.
Remember that as a programmer you are also a stakeholder. Your reputation is on the line.
Yes, I know, UX is not your job. But do it anyway; because your name is on that product.
key_insight: Programmers must take personal ownership of usability because their name is on the product, regardless of whether UX is formally their job.
